key_insight: Finite-element modeling linked hardness-derived material properties to predict stress and strain hot spots in 36-year-old and as-received X52 pipeline welds.
engineering_relevance: The results help judge whether aging gas-pipeline welded joints can still support safe operation or repurposing for hydrogen service.
method: Measured hardness across weld metal, heat-affected zone, and base metal.
Estimated strength and plasticity from hardness using correlation equations and built zone-specific stress–strain curves.
Simulated tensile loading in FEM to map stress/strain distributions and identify peak-stress regions.
evidence_signal: The operated weld showed a higher normalized peak stress ratio (σ11/σys = 1.25) than the as-received joint (1.1), and strain concentration occurred at loads below base-metal yield in both cases.
watch_item: Hardness-based property estimation and simulated tensile loading should be validated against broader experimental datasets before using the model for life-extension decisions.
paper_title: Assessment of the Strength of Welded Joints in a Gas Pipeline Using FEM Modeling
